title: Building a React Page Builder — End to End
topic: How to build an Elementor-style page creator in React — architecture, component registry, canvas renderer, drag layer (@dnd-kit nested zones), props panel, JSON serialisation, editor shell, and integration with the existing Vector design system, Samantha SDK, and user_custom_pages JSONB storage.
summary: End-to-end architecture for a first-party React drag-and-drop page builder: registry, canvas, @dnd-kit drag layer, props panel, Samantha SDK data binding, and publish flow.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Component Registry
  4. 4. JSON Tree Schema
  5. 5. Canvas Renderer
  6. 6. Drag Layer — @dnd-kit
  7. 7. Props Panel
  8. 8. Editor Shell
  9. 9. Samantha SDK Integration
  10. 10. Vector Design System Fit
  11. 11. Storage and API
  12. 12. Build Sequence
  13. 13. Risks and Mitigations

1. Overview

This paper defines the end-to-end architecture for a first-party React page builder embedded in the MMFF Vector product. The goal is an Elementor-style drag-and-drop canvas that lets padmins and (optionally) users compose custom pages from a curated palette of Vector components. Page state is serialised to a typed JSON tree stored in the user_custom_pages.content JSONB column and rendered server-side or client-side via a recursive renderer.

Because we own the full stack — design system, data SDK, storage schema, and component library — we build our own rather than adopt Craft.js or GrapesJS. Third-party builders bring serialisation opacity, bundle weight, and opinionated node models that fight our existing conventions. Building our own means the palette, the JSON schema, and the migration path are ours permanently.

The six moving parts are: Component Registry, JSON Tree, Canvas Renderer, Drag Layer, Props Panel, and Editor Shell. Each is described in full below, followed by the integration contracts for the Samantha SDK and Vector design system, the storage/API layer, a recommended build sequence, and a risk register.

2. Key Findings

  • The existing user_custom_pages table and /p/<uuid> route are the correct storage and routing targets — no schema changes needed for phase 1.
  • @dnd-kit is the recommended drag primitive. It handles nested drop zones, keyboard accessibility, and pointer/touch events out of the box. Its modular architecture means we only import what we use. It is MIT-licensed and has no peer dependency conflicts with Next.js 14.
  • The component registry pattern — a plain TypeScript map of type → descriptor — is the single best architectural decision. It keeps the canvas renderer, props panel, and palette all driven from one source of truth.
  • The JSON tree schema should be flat with parent references (not nested), making partial updates, undo/redo, and drag reordering O(1) operations on a node map rather than deep tree mutations.
  • Samantha SDK components are a natural fit as data-bound palette entries — a SamanthaField component reads from samantha.portfolio.* at render time, keeping the page tree data-free (no values baked in, only field references).
  • The Vector design system requires zero adaptation — palette components are built from existing globals.css classes and CSS variables, so every dropped component inherits the active theme automatically.
  • A publish/draft split on user_custom_pages is required before the editor ships. A published_content column (or status flag) separates what visitors see from what the editor is working on.

3. Component Registry

The registry is the spine of the entire system. Every draggable component is registered once; the canvas renderer, palette, and props panel all derive their behaviour from it.

Registry entry shape

interface ComponentDescriptor<P extends Record<string, unknown> = Record<string, unknown>> {
  type: string;                        // unique key, e.g. "text-block"
  label: string;                       // shown in palette
  icon: string;                        // CSS class or SVG name
  defaultProps: P;                     // used when dropped onto canvas
  propsSchema: PropField[];            // drives the props panel
  canHaveChildren: boolean;            // true for layout containers
  allowedParents?: string[];           // restrict nesting (optional)
  component: React.ComponentType<P>;  // the actual renderer
  previewComponent?: React.ComponentType<P>; // lightweight palette thumbnail
}

PropField schema

type PropField =
  | { key: string; label: string; type: "text" | "textarea" | "color" | "select"; options?: string[] }
  | { key: string; label: string; type: "samantha-field"; namespace: string }
  | { key: string; label: string; type: "toggle" }
  | { key: string; label: string; type: "number"; min?: number; max?: number };

Initial palette — phase 1

TypeLabelCan have childrenNotes
sectionSectionYesTop-level layout block, full width
columnsColumnsYes1–4 column grid; children are column slots
text-blockTextNoRich-ish text: bold, italic, links
headingHeadingNoh2/h3/h4, style variants
dividerDividerNoHorizontal rule with spacing props
metric-cardMetric CardNoLabel + value, driven by Samantha field ref
layer-badgeLayer BadgeNoRenders a subscription layer tag + name pill
data-tableData TableNoTabular Samantha data, column config in props
chart-embedChartNoReference to a chart component by slug
custom-htmlHTML BlockNoPadmin-only; raw HTML passthrough

4. JSON Tree Schema

The page is stored as a flat node map plus a root array. Flat is better than nested because drag reorder, undo/redo, and partial PATCH are all O(1) map operations rather than recursive tree mutations.

interface PageTree {
  root: string[];           // ordered list of top-level node IDs
  nodes: Record<string, PageNode>;
}

interface PageNode {
  id: string;               // nanoid(), stable across sessions
  type: string;             // must match a registry key
  props: Record<string, unknown>;
  children: string[];       // ordered child node IDs (empty for leaf nodes)
  parentId: string | null;
}

Example — a two-column section

{
  "root": ["n1"],
  "nodes": {
    "n1": { "id": "n1", "type": "section",  "props": { "paddingY": 4 }, "children": ["n2"], "parentId": null },
    "n2": { "id": "n2", "type": "columns", "props": { "count": 2 },     "children": ["n3", "n4"], "parentId": "n1" },
    "n3": { "id": "n3", "type": "heading",  "props": { "text": "Hello", "level": "h2" }, "children": [], "parentId": "n2" },
    "n4": { "id": "n4", "type": "metric-card", "props": { "fieldRef": "samantha.portfolio.layerCount" }, "children": [], "parentId": "n2" }
  }
}

Undo/redo

Store a history: PageTree[] stack in a useReducer. Every mutation pushes a shallow clone of the node map. Max depth 50. Undo pops; redo uses a separate forward stack. Because the tree is flat, cloning is a single { ...nodes } spread — no deep copy needed.

5. Canvas Renderer

The canvas is a recursive React component that walks the node tree and renders each node via its registered component. In edit mode it wraps every node in a selection/drag shell; in view mode (the /p/<uuid> public route) it renders bare components with no editor chrome.

function CanvasNode({ nodeId, editMode }: { nodeId: string; editMode: boolean }) {
  const node = usePageNode(nodeId);           // reads from tree context
  const descriptor = registry[node.type];
  const Component = descriptor.component;

  const children = node.children.map((childId) => (
    <CanvasNode key={childId} nodeId={childId} editMode={editMode} />
  ));

  if (!editMode) {
    return <Component {...node.props}>{children}</Component>;
  }

  return (
    <NodeShell nodeId={nodeId}>         {/* selection border, drag handle, delete */}
      <Component {...node.props}>{children}</Component>
    </NodeShell>
  );
}

NodeShell responsibilities (edit mode only)

  • 1px accent border on hover/selection
  • Floating toolbar: move up, move down, duplicate, delete
  • Drag handle (passed to @dnd-kit useDraggable)
  • Click → dispatch SELECT_NODE to editor context (opens props panel)

Drop zones

Nodes with canHaveChildren: true render a DropZone between each child and at the start/end of the children list. A DropZone is a thin useDroppable target that accepts a dragged node or a palette item. On drop it dispatches MOVE_NODE or INSERT_NODE to the tree reducer.

6. Drag Layer — @dnd-kit

@dnd-kit/core + @dnd-kit/sortable handle all drag interaction. It uses the Pointer Events API (not HTML5 drag-and-drop) which gives us full control over drag previews, cancel on Escape, and touch support.

Key setup

// Wrap the entire editor in DndContext
<DndContext
  sensors={sensors}              // PointerSensor + KeyboardSensor
  collisionDetection={closestCenter}
  onDragStart={handleDragStart}
  onDragOver={handleDragOver}    // show drop indicator
  onDragEnd={handleDragEnd}      // commit move or insert
>
  <Canvas />
  <DragOverlay>                  // floating ghost during drag
    {activeId && <DragGhost nodeId={activeId} />}
  </DragOverlay>
</DndContext>

Two drag sources

  1. Palette item — dragged from the sidebar. On drop into a valid zone, dispatches INSERT_NODE with a fresh node built from defaultProps.
  2. Canvas node — dragged from its handle. On drop into a different zone, dispatches MOVE_NODE (detach from old parent, insert at new position).

Nested drop zone disambiguation

When a dragged item hovers over nested containers, multiple useDroppable zones fire onDragOver. Resolve with a deepest-first rule: collect all active droppable IDs, sort by DOM depth (use element.closest count), take the innermost. This prevents accidental drops on ancestor containers when the user intends a child zone.

Preventing invalid nesting

In onDragOver, check the dragged node's type against the drop target's allowedParents and the target's canHaveChildren flag. If invalid, set a dropForbidden flag on state and style the zone with a danger indicator. onDragEnd is a no-op when dropForbidden is true.

7. Props Panel

When a node is selected, the right sidebar renders its editable props by iterating the registry descriptor's propsSchema array. Each PropField type maps to a control:

PropField typeControl
text<input type="text"> — inline edit, commits on blur
textarea<textarea> — auto-grow, commits on blur
colorCSS variable picker — dropdown of --accent, --surface, etc.
select<select> with supplied options array
toggleCheckbox styled as a toggle
number<input type="number"> with min/max
samantha-fieldSearchable dropdown of available samantha.* field paths

Every control dispatches UPDATE_NODE_PROP on change. Because the tree is flat, this is a single map key update — no structural mutation, no re-render cascade outside the affected node.

Panel sections

  • Content — primary text/data props
  • Layout — spacing, alignment, width props
  • Style — colour, typography variant props
  • Advanced — visibility rules, custom CSS class (padmin only)

8. Editor Shell

The editor shell is the padmin-only page that hosts the canvas, palette, and props panel. Route: /settings/pages/[id]/edit.

Layout

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Toolbar: page title | Undo | Redo | Preview | Save  │
├──────────┬──────────────────────────┬────────────────┤
│ Palette  │        Canvas            │  Props Panel   │
│ (200px)  │  (flex 1, scrollable)    │  (280px)       │
│          │                          │                │
│ sections │  ┌──────────────────┐    │  [node props]  │
│ layout   │  │  NodeShell       │    │                │
│ content  │  │  ┌────────────┐  │    │                │
│ data     │  │  │ Component  │  │    │                │
│ charts   │  │  └────────────┘  │    │                │
│          │  └──────────────────┘    │                │
└──────────┴──────────────────────────┴────────────────┘

Toolbar actions

  • Undo/Redo — dispatch to history reducer
  • Preview — open /p/<uuid> in a new tab (reads published draft)
  • Save draft — PATCH content column, no visibility change
  • Publish — PATCH published_content + set status = published; requires type-to-confirm if page already live

State management

A single useReducer owns the page tree and history stack. Actions: INSERT_NODE, MOVE_NODE, DELETE_NODE, UPDATE_NODE_PROP, SELECT_NODE, UNDO, REDO. The reducer is pure — no side effects. Auto-save fires a debounced PATCH (2s) after any tree mutation, writing to content (draft) only.

9. Samantha SDK Integration

Data-bound components (metric-card, data-table, layer-badge) do not store values in the page tree — they store field references. At render time the component calls into the Samantha SDK to resolve the value.

// metric-card component
function MetricCard({ fieldRef, label }: { fieldRef: string; label: string }) {
  const value = samantha.portfolio.getValue(fieldRef);
  return (
    <div className="metric-card">
      <span className="metric-card__label">{label}</span>
      <span className="metric-card__value">{value ?? '—'}</span>
    </div>
  );
}

The samantha-field prop type in the props panel renders a searchable dropdown populated by samantha.portfolio.getSchema() — the full list of available field paths for the current subscription. This means the editor never needs to know what data exists; Samantha exposes it dynamically.

In editor preview mode, Samantha returns stub/sanitised values so the canvas renders realistic-looking content without exposing live data during editing.

10. Vector Design System Fit

Every palette component is built from existing globals.css classes and CSS variables. No component introduces a style={{}} prop or hardcoded colour value. This means:

  • The active theme (light/dark, accent colour) applies automatically to all dropped components
  • The color prop field type offers only CSS variable tokens, not hex values — keeping pages on-brand
  • Typography uses var(--font-sans) and text size tokens (var(--text-sm) etc.) exclusively
  • Spacing props map to var(--space-N) tokens, not pixel values

The palette sidebar itself uses the existing .sidebar shell pattern. The props panel uses .form and .form__field classes. The toolbar uses .btn + variant. Zero new CSS classes are needed for the editor chrome in phase 1 — only the component-specific classes for palette items are new.

11. Storage and API

Schema additions required

TableColumnTypePurpose
user_custom_pagescontentjsonbDraft page tree (already exists)
user_custom_pagespublished_contentjsonbLive page tree — new, null until first publish
user_custom_pagesstatustextdraft | published | archived — new
user_custom_pagesschema_versionintPage tree version for future migrations — new

API endpoints

MethodPathPurpose
GET/api/pagesList padmin's pages (id, title, status, updated_at)
POST/api/pagesCreate new page (returns id)
GET/api/pages/:idLoad draft tree for editor
PATCH/api/pages/:idSave draft content (auto-save target)
POST/api/pages/:id/publishCopy draft to published_content, set status
GET/api/pages/:id/publicRead published_content for /p/:uuid renderer (no auth)

12. Build Sequence

Recommended story order — each phase is independently shippable:

  1. Phase 1 — Foundation: schema migration (published_content, status, schema_version), component registry module, JSON tree reducer + context, bare canvas renderer (view mode only, no drag)
  2. Phase 2 — Editor shell: editor route, palette sidebar, NodeShell selection chrome, props panel (text/select/toggle controls only), save draft API wiring
  3. Phase 3 — Drag layer: @dnd-kit integration, palette drag-to-canvas, canvas node reorder, nested drop zone with deepest-first resolution, invalid nesting guard
  4. Phase 4 — Data binding: samantha-field prop type, metric-card and layer-badge components, Samantha stub values in editor preview mode
  5. Phase 5 — Publish flow: publish endpoint, published_content renderer on /p/:uuid, type-to-confirm publish gate, page list management UI
  6. Phase 6 — Advanced palette: data-table, chart-embed, columns layout, custom-html (padmin only), undo/redo history stack

13. Risks and Mitigations

RiskSeverityMitigation
Nested drop zone disambiguation is complex to get rightHighIsolate in a single useDropResolver hook, unit-test with mocked DOM depths before wiring to canvas
JSON tree schema changes break existing pagesHighStore schema_version from day one; write a migration runner that transforms old trees on read
Samantha SDK not ready when editor builds startMedPhase 4 is deliberately late; phases 1–3 use static prop values only
Canvas performance with many nodesMedEach CanvasNode is a separate React component — React reconciliation handles this well. Virtualise only if node count exceeds ~200 in practice
XSS via custom-html componentHighSanitise with DOMPurify on both save and render; restrict custom-html to padmin role only; CSP header on /p/* routes
Undo history grows unboundedLowCap at 50 entries; older entries are silently dropped
